title: The enforcement discretion
paras: Let's begin with the enforcement discretion. Section 172 of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 empowers an authority — it never obliges one — to issue an enforcement notice where it appears there has been a breach and it is expedient to issue it, having regard to the development plan and other material considerations. The N-P-P-F requires authorities to act proportionately in responding to suspected breaches. Now, expedient doesn't mean convenient, politically comfortable, or popular with the complainant. It means the action serves a legitimate planning purpose, the harm justifies the resource, and the outcome sought is achievable and proportionate. Before any action, apply the public interest test: what planning harm is this breach causing, and does that harm justify action? The system isn't designed to punish the absence of a piece of paper.

Not acting is sometimes exactly the right decision — and it takes more confidence than serving a notice. The marginal extension that's well designed, harms nobody and would be granted on application should draw a letter inviting a retrospective application. And if none comes and there's no material harm, a recorded judgment that action is not expedient, and a closed file. But the discretion is not cover for inaction. Unauthorised works to a listed building, a felled protected tree, real harm to Green Belt openness, the commercial use generating continuous disturbance — these demand prompt action. The officer who spends six months negotiating while the breach beds in isn't exercising discretion. They're allowing a problem to worsen.

Negotiation resolves the majority of breaches, but only with discipline: a clear objective, a defined timetable, and the willingness to end it when it stops producing progress. A deadline that isn't enforced is not a deadline. The developer who watches a stated date pass without consequence has learned that the authority's options apparently include doing nothing. So state what is expected and by when, make the timetable realistic for what's being asked, and mean it. And the distinction between the householder who genuinely didn't know and the developer running the clock on a twice-refused site — that decides which conversation to have.

However the discretion is exercised, record it — clearly, specifically, in terms a stranger could follow in two years. A decision not to act should set out the breach, the harm or its absence, the policy position, the representations, and why action is not expedient. "No action — not expedient" defends nothing when the complainant reaches the ombudsman, who assesses process, communication and transparency rather than planning merits. Consistency matters too. Declining to act on one extension creates a practical precedent, and the officer reaching a different view on the next one needs a recorded reason why the cases differ. A clear enforcement policy, and a prioritisation framework — high, medium, low, with defined response times that are genuinely applied — are what make like cases alike.

title: Breach, immunity & evidence
paras: Now, breach, immunity and evidence. Section 171A defines a breach as development without the required permission, or failure to comply with a condition or limitation. Neither is a criminal offence. Mainstream enforcement is a discretionary civil regime, and the officer who treats the landowner as a suspect and the site as a crime scene has misunderstood the job. But the exceptions matter as much as the rule. Unauthorised works to a listed building are a crime from the moment they're executed — that's section 9 of the Listed Buildings Act: no notice needed, no time limit, penalties unlimited, and the harm often irreversible. Displaying an advertisement without consent is an offence with a continuing daily element. Works to a tree protected by a tree preservation order are an absolute offence under section 210 — ignorance of the order is no defence. And removing a protected countryside hedgerow without notification under the Hedgerows Regulations 1997 is an offence — one that catches Class Q conversions out regularly. So categorise civil or criminal first, because it defines the tools, the tone and the procedure.

Before reaching for any tool, establish that there's a breach at all. Check the General Permitted Development Order — the G-P-D-O — before anything else. If the extension is permitted development, the investigation is over, and proceeding against permitted development is an embarrassing and expensive retreat. Internal works, maintenance, changes within a use class, de minimis matters and agricultural use of land are not development, however many complaints arrive. On material change of use, identify the planning unit correctly before anything else — a farm may be one unit or several, and the wrong unit produces a notice alleging the wrong breach on the wrong land. And keep lawfulness distinct from permission. A use that's immune through the passage of time is lawful by immunity, not approved. A certificate of lawfulness of existing use or development — a CLEUD — confirms the enforcement window has closed, nothing more.

The time limits changed fundamentally on the 25th of April 2024. Section 115 of the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 replaced the four-year rule, so all breaches occurring on or after that date carry a single ten-year period. The transitional provisions create a dual-track regime for years to come. Operational development substantially completed before the 25th of April 2024, and changes of use to a single dwellinghouse occurring before that date, keep the four-year limit — and misapplying the split loses the case on ground D, with no remedy at appeal. Substantial completion means capable of use for its intended purpose, not fully finished — so the clock can be running while ancillary works are deliberately prolonged. Deliberate concealment — positive steps to hide the breach, not mere failure to advertise it — can disapply the limits via a Planning Enforcement Order from the magistrates, within six months of sufficient knowledge, giving an enforcement year. And the second-bite provision, section 171B(4)(b), allows further action within four years of previous enforcement action where a notice proved defective. That's a safety net, not a habit.

Investigation begins at a desk, not on a doorstep. Planning history, conditions, previous enforcement, land registry — serve the right people — aerial imagery and historic mapping, where the photographs showing bare land in 2019 defeat the claim of fifteen years' use, and the policy constraints that shape the expediency judgment. On site: systematic date-stamped photographs stored on authority systems, laser measurements, not estimates, contemporaneous signed notes, and a careful record of what's said voluntarily — the date works started, who did them, what was intended. And go beyond the obvious sources: building control records, licensing, environmental health, council tax and the electoral register — often determinative of which time limit applies — and social media or review histories that date a use precisely. The case file is the product. Everything else — the notice, the appeal statement, the prosecution — is only as good as the file it's drawn from.

title: The Planning Contravention Notice
paras: Next, the planning contravention notice. The P-C-N — that's section 171C of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 — may be served on an owner, occupier, or person with an interest in or carrying out operations on land, where it appears a breach may have occurred. The threshold is deliberately low: may, not has, not probably has. It's an investigative tool for when the authority needs more information, not a conclusion-drawing exercise for cases already established. And it's discretionary. There's no obligation to serve one before enforcing, and where the breach is clear and delay would let the situation worsen or the clock run, proceeding straight to a notice is entirely proper.

It requires the recipient to state what operations have been carried out, what the land is used for, and whether conditions have been complied with — and it can invite them to say how any suspected breach might be resolved. The recipient has 21 days. Failure to respond is a criminal offence under section 171D, as is a false or misleading response. In practice, the sanction for silence is rarely invoked, and the calculating recipient sometimes answers minimally or not at all. In which case the investigation proceeds by other means. The P-C-N fills gaps — it is not a gateway that must be passed.

It's not available for suspected breaches of the listed building, conservation area, hazardous substances or tree regimes, which carry their own investigative powers — a P-C-N served on a listed building case covers no bases at all. Nor can it compel self-incrimination where conduct could give rise to criminal liability. Where the case is criminal in character, the PACE framework governs, and the P-C-N route is not appropriate. And unlike enforcement notices, stop notices and breach of condition notices, a P-C-N is not entered in the enforcement register, is not a land charge, and doesn't bind a purchaser — so if the land changes hands mid-investigation, consider a fresh notice on the new owner.

Its best work is done in change of use cases where the nature or history of a use is unclear, in condition cases where the extent of non-compliance needs establishing, and in cases where ownership or responsibility is uncertain. It's least useful — sometimes counterproductive — against clearly visible, clearly unauthorised operational development, where its only effect is to alert the recipient and hand them three weeks to prepare. A signed response is evidence. The landowner who tells the authority one commencement date in a P-C-N response, and a different one at appeal, has an inconsistency to explain to an inspector.

title: Enforcement notices: drafting to survive
paras: Now to enforcement notices, and drafting to survive. Section 172 requires both elements — a breach, and the expediency judgment — and the decision must be properly authorised. Usually that's under delegated powers, on a report setting out the breach, the harm, the merits and the expediency reasons, with committee authorisation considered where the case is politically sensitive or finely balanced. Section 173 dictates the contents. The land — a plan on an Ordnance Survey base, site edged red, because a verbal description invites argument about scope. The matters constituting the breach. The steps required, or the activities to cease. The reasons why action is expedient. And the effective date and compliance period. Each element got wrong is a ground of appeal.

The breach description is where more notices fail than anywhere else. It must be precise enough that the recipient knows exactly what's alleged. Accurate, in that it describes what actually happened. In plain English — a section 179 prosecution can fail if a court finds the notice incomprehensible to a lay person. And complete: in mixed-use cases, all the uses on the land must be identified, because section 173(11) deems permission granted for what a complied-with notice leaves standing. Here's the worked trap. A notice alleging only the unauthorised vehicle-repair use, on a site whose residential use was also never authorised, may, on compliance, deem permission for the very residential use the authority would have refused. So identify everything on the site, then decide deliberately what to allege and what to leave.

The requirements are governed by the Mansi principle: compliance must leave the land as it was before the breach — no better and, critically, no worse. Establish what existed before the breach, what changed because of it, and require precisely the difference. A notice loose enough to catch pre-existing development over-enforces, and one requiring reinstatement beyond what the alleged breach logically demands does the same in reverse. Deliberate under-enforcement, under section 173(3), is legitimate — requiring removal of the offending storey rather than the whole building — provided the deemed-permission consequence for what remains is a considered and recorded choice, never a drafting accident. And the requirements must match the breach description exactly. Mismatch between the two is the commonest avoidable drafting failure.

Compliance periods are tested against what a reasonable person would need, not what the authority would prefer. Weeks for a small structure or a clean cessation. Months for substantial demolition or business relocation. And stepped periods where a use must stop quickly but removal can follow. Then service: on the owner, the occupier and anyone with a materially affected interest — known mortgagees included — not more than 28 days after issue, and not less than 28 days before the effective date, with the statutory explanatory note on appeal rights. Do the land registry search immediately before service, not from last year's planning history. And if an appeal is lodged before the effective date, the notice is suspended until determination — which is precisely why a technically robust notice, unattractive to appeal on drafting grounds, saves months.

title: Stop notices & urgent intervention
paras: Stop notices, and urgent intervention. The stop notice, under section 183, is an accelerant attached to an enforcement notice — it cannot stand alone. It prohibits specified activity within as little as three days of service, or immediately, where special reasons — urgent environmental harm, irreversible heritage damage, public safety — are stated and served with it. It may not prohibit the use of a building as a dwellinghouse. Though it may prohibit use of land as a site for a residential caravan — a distinction that sits close to the human rights arguments in traveller cases. Contravention is an offence carrying an unlimited fine, on summary conviction or on indictment, with the court required to have regard to the financial benefit accrued. And that provision matters, because the profit from continuing often exceeds any modest fine.

The compensation risk, under section 186, is real, but historically overstated. Compensation arises where the enforcement notice is quashed on grounds other than the planning merits — technical or legal failure — or is varied or withdrawn otherwise than on a grant of permission. Where an inspector quashes the notice by granting permission on a ground A appeal, compensation is not automatically payable, and section 186(5) bars compensation for any activity that was, while the stop notice was in force, a breach of planning control. The authority that has its fundamentals right — a genuine breach, correct time limits, proper service — carries far less exposure than the historic caution suggested. The real exposure comes from fundamental error: stopping activity that was never a breach, was immune, or was defectively served.

The temporary stop notice, under section 171E, is the more practical first response. It's freestanding — no prior enforcement notice needed — effective immediately on display, and, since the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act, from the 25th of April 2024, it lasts 56 days rather than 28. That converts it from a scramble into genuine breathing room to investigate and draft properly. Its limits, though. It's prohibitory only — it cannot require demolition or reinstatement. It cannot prohibit use of a building as a dwellinghouse. Compensation, under section 171H, arises only in four situations — an existing permission complied with, permitted development, a subsequent lawful development certificate, or withdrawal otherwise than on a grant. And no second temporary stop notice can be served on the same activity without intervening enforcement action — so a temporary stop notice allowed to expire without follow-through forfeits the immediate-intervention route. Section 103 of the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act added the listed building temporary stop notice: where unauthorised works to a listed building are suspected, halting them for 56 days is now the first tool to reach for, because the harm is too often permanent to wait.

When the notices have been served, upheld and ignored, three escalations remain. First, prosecution under section 179 for non-compliance: an unlimited fine, on summary conviction or on indictment — the former £20,000 magistrates' cap was removed in March 2015, by LASPO 2012 and S-I 2015 number 664 — with financial benefit reflected in the penalty. And it's chargeable for successive periods, so the developer who continues after conviction can be prosecuted again and again, until non-compliance is dearer than compliance. Second, direct action under section 178: entering the land, carrying out the required steps, and recovering the costs. And third, the injunction under section 187B — the system's remedy of last resort in all but the most urgent cases — for persistent deliberate defiance, irreversible harm in progress, or credibly apprehended breaches before they start. Its force is simple: breach of an injunction is contempt of court, and contempt carries imprisonment. Courts have generally not required undertakings in damages from authorities acting in the public interest — so advice dismissing the route on that ground deserves scepticism.

title: Enforcement appeals: the grounds
paras: Finally, enforcement appeals, and the grounds. Appeal lies under section 174 on seven grounds, and each one corresponds to a vulnerability the notice should have been drafted against. Ground A: permission ought to be granted. That's the planning merits ground, operating as a retrospective application, with a fee that lapses the ground if it isn't paid — and the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act curtailed its availability from the 25th of April 2024, reducing its use as a pure delaying tactic. Ground B: the matters alleged have not occurred — a factual challenge the authority must meet on the balance of probabilities, with the evidence the investigation gathered. Ground C: the matters do not constitute a breach — the legal-analysis challenge that exposes a flawed categorisation. And ground D: out of time — now dominated by the 25th of April 2024 transitional split, and unanswerable at appeal if the limits were misapplied.

Ground E: defective service — not automatically fatal, correctable where no injustice results, but serious where someone with a material interest was deprived of the chance to appeal. Ground F: the requirements exceed what's necessary — Mansi territory, where over-enforcement is trimmed by the inspector. And ground G: the compliance period is too short — the least contentious ground, readily granted, and telling about the care taken in drafting. Anyone with an interest in the land, or any relevant occupier, may appeal — served or not — which is why pre-service investigation of interests prevents surprises. The appeal must be received before the notice takes effect. There is no discretion to accept a late enforcement appeal, and lodging suspends the notice, and the compliance clock, until determination.

There are three procedures. Written representations for straightforward cases. Hearings where structured discussion assists. And inquiries for complex cases needing cross-examination — with the authority entitled to argue for the simpler procedure where the appellant has requested more than the case warrants. The statement of case must address every ground raised, not just the comfortable ones, supported by the file evidence. And the Inspectorate's timetable is not optional. Late submission can exclude evidence, and in serious cases of delay the inspector can quash the notice for procedural failure alone — an avoidable, and public, humiliation. The inspector can dismiss, quash, correct misdescriptions where no injustice results, or vary the notice. But a notice so fundamentally defective that correction would produce a substantially different notice cannot be saved.

Costs follow unreasonable behaviour, not the event — and they run both ways. Against the authority: a notice clearly unsustainable on the merits, grounds of resistance with no realistic prospect, late or inadequate evidence, allegations the evidence doesn't support. Against the appellant: unmeritorious grounds, failure to engage, unreasonable delay — and authorities should apply for costs where conduct warrants it. Then, after the decision. If the appeal is dismissed, the compliance period runs from the decision date — diarise it and monitor, because the dismissed appeal begins the compliance phase rather than ending the case. If the notice is quashed, decide promptly whether a fresh notice is possible, whether time has run, and whether the site has changed. And challenge beyond the inspector lies only to the High Court, on a point of law, under section 289 — within 28 days.
